retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Preoperative Management -- Issues of Concern -- Pregnancy Testing
retrieved_docs.content: The pregnancy rate in teenage girls continues to fall in the US, but it remains a dilemma for anesthesiologists. At least 5% of girls become pregnant before the age of 19. A significant number of these pregnancies are unsuspected and are only discovered when the patient presents for surgery. Once a child is found to be pregnant, she legally becomes an emancipated minor, and the information about her health cannot be shared with anyone without her permission. This is often a very challenging task for the operating room staff when the parents and other family members are sitting in the room. The diagnosis of pregnancy may also be the result of rape or incest, and therefore the possibility of criminal activity needs to be addressed. If a patient is found to be pregnant, elective surgery will almost always.

retrieved_docs.title: Retinopathy of Prematurity -- Postoperative and Rehabilitation Care -- Postoperative and Rehabilitation Care
retrieved_docs.content: Early intervention referral: Coordinate with developmental pediatrics for vision-specific early intervention services, such as orientation and mobility training and vision therapy. Physical and occupational therapy: Address motor delays common in preterm infants, supporting hand-eye coordination and postural stability. [144]

retrieved_docs.title: Perioperative Management of Patients With Congenital Heart Disease -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: There is fetal wastage due to congenital heart defects incompatible with life and some heart lesions that remain asymptomatic (e.g., bicuspid aortic valve and patent ductus arteriosus) in early life, thus making the true incidence unknown. The different population shows a different pattern of CHD. For instance, there is an increased prevalence of congenital disabilities in premature and small for gestational age infants compared to full-term newborns. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Gestational Age Assessment -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: All pregnant patients should have a gestational age calculated for safe prenatal evaluation throughout the remainder of the pregnancy for both mother and fetus.

retrieved_docs.title: Gestational Age Assessment -- Complications -- Sonographic Methods for Determining Gestational Age
retrieved_docs.content: Ossification Centers: These parameters do not directly correlate with exact gestational age. Presence of these ossified centers are rather markers of fetal maturity which is seen later in pregnancy. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Preterm and Term Prelabor Rupture of Membranes (PPROM and PROM) -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Assess gestational age and fetal lung maturity to determine the safest course of action regarding delivery or expectant management. Apply current guidelines for monitoring fetal well-being and signs of infection during conservative management of prelabor rupture of membranes. Select appropriate timing for delivery based on maternal and fetal status, balancing risks and benefits to minimize complications. Apply interprofessional team strategies to improve care coordination and outcomes in patients with prelabor rupture of membranes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Pregnancy Dating -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Establishing an accurate gestational age and estimated delivery date is the most important step in managing any pregnancy. [1] Accurate knowledge of gestational age allows laboratory and screening tests to be performed at the appropriate time in the pregnancy. [1] Optimal dating before 22 0/7 weeks enables an accurate assessment of fetal growth as the pregnancy progresses. Suboptimally dated pregnancies, due to the error of ultrasound at advanced gestational age, can be difficult to manage because of the uncertainty of pregnancy dating. Elective delivery should not be performed in suboptimally dated pregnancies. In pregnancies with clear medical indications for delivery (pre-eclampsia, gestational diabetes, etc), delivery planning should be based on the best clinical estimation of gestational age. Amniocentesis for fetal lung maturity should not be used routinely before planning delivery for sub-optimally dated pregnancy. Even with proven fetal lung maturity, late preterm and early-term infants have an increased risk of respiratory morbidity. Elective delivery should be performed at 41 completed weeks due to concerns that the fetus could be further along than estimated by third-trimester ultrasound. Antepartum fetal testing can be performed after 39 weeks in patients with sub-optimal dating due to concerns for post-term pregnancy. [3] In a sub-optimally dated pregnancy, a repeat low transverse cesarean delivery, if desired by the patient, should be performed at 39 weeks based on the best estimate of gestational age. [1] [6] [7] [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Preterm and Term Prelabor Rupture of Membranes (PPROM and PROM) -- Treatment / Management -- Previable and periviable (<24 weeks of gestation)
retrieved_docs.content: Notably, expectant management in previable and periviable situations is generally not associated with positive neonatal outcomes. In a European series of patients known as the PPROMEXIL-III cohort, 52 of 86 (60%) patients with previable PROM were delivered before 24 weeks, and only 23 neonates survived until discharge. Long-term data showed that 13 of these children (15% of the original 86) had normal neurodevelopment at 2 and 5 years. [47] This is similar to the results reported by Sklar et al, who found only 15.7% of women in the expectant management group avoided morbidity and had a neonate who survived until discharge. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Estimated Date of Delivery(Archived) -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The determination of gestational age is vital to providing quality prenatal care. Knowing the gestational age allows appropriate care for the mother without compromising maternal or fetal status. Knowing the gestation age allows for the correct timing of management, such as administering steroids for fetal lung maturity, starting ASA therapy with a history of pre-eclampsia in previous pregnancies, and starting Makena for previous preterm deliveries.

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Preoperative Management -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The preoperative management of anesthesia in children differs from that employed in adults, including employing age-group appropriate language, understanding the dynamics of the child's family, and assessing the patient's developmental stage. Children are not small adults. Hence, we must have an understanding of the variability of each child's age and family-specific needs in an effort to develop a customized anesthesia management plan. The anesthesiologist must appreciate the stress the patients and their families may be experiencing. The anesthesiologist must review the child's presenting medical and psychological conditions. In this chapter, the clinical significance and potential strategies for enhancing pediatric healthcare outcomes will be discussed.

retrieved_docs.title: Sonography Assessment of Gestational Age -- Technique or Treatment -- First Trimester
retrieved_docs.content: Gestational sac:  The gestational sac can first be seen at around 5 weeks on a transvaginal examination and 6 weeks on a transabdominal examination. It grows approximately 1 mm per day, and measurements become less accurate once it reaches a size of 14 mm. [2] [6] The gestational age of the embryo is determined by measuring the mean diameter of the sac as measured from inside wall to inside wall of the sac.  If the gestational sac is not completely round, then it may be measured using three orthogonal distances from the inside of the sac using sagittal and transverse views. [1] The mean sac diameter is then either compared to a table of values, input into a regression model, or input into the ultrasound machine, determining the estimated age through pre-set calculators on an "Obstetric" setting. [7] [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Preterm and Term Prelabor Rupture of Membranes (PPROM and PROM) -- Treatment / Management -- Previable and periviable (<24 weeks of gestation)
retrieved_docs.content: Clinicians should note that maternal sepsis can progress rapidly to death. For example, a study on maternal mortality in France provided results that found the median interval from PPROM to the first signs of infection was 5 days; the median time from infection diagnosis to death was only 18 hours. [46] A delay in uterine evacuation was determined to have contributed to at least some of the deaths in that series.

retrieved_docs.title: Cervical Cancer -- Surgical Oncology -- Cervical Cancer in Pregnancy
retrieved_docs.content: The determination of gestational age is crucial. Minimum fetal viability is approximately 24 weeks gestation, but there is a significant risk of neonatal death or long-term disability in those who survive. Disability-free survival at 25 years increases substantially with advancing gestational age, with 4% of infants born at 22 weeks versus 78% for those born at 28 weeks gestation. In contrast, full-term infants have a disability-free survival of 97%. [43]

retrieved_docs.title: Retinopathy of Prematurity -- Treatment Planning
retrieved_docs.content: Effective ROP management hinges on timely screening, precise staging, and individualized intervention strategies, all coordinated by an interprofessional team comprising neonatologists, ophthalmologists, nursing staff, and, when needed, pediatric anesthesiologists (see Table. Retinopathy of Prematurity Treatment Modalities). Once an infant meets screening criteria (eg, birth weight ≤1500 g or gestational age ≤30 weeks, or as per local guidelines), dilated indirect ophthalmoscopy should occur at 4 to 6 weeks’ postnatal age or 31 to 33 weeks’ postmenstrual age—whichever is later. Disease is classified by zone (I–III), stage (1–5), and the presence of “plus” disease, as per the ICROP. [31]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494
retrieved_docs.content: A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is associated with a liveborn infant, indicating that pregnancies reaching this stage can result in a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1697
retrieved_docs.content: A caesarean section scar of unknown type and a poor reproductive history are clinically associated with مخاض قبل الأوان (preterm labor), indicating that women who have an indeterminate type of previous cesarean scar or who have experienced suboptimal reproductive outcomes are at increased risk for premature labor. Recognizing these associations enables clinicians to identify high‑risk patients, implement closer surveillance, and consider preventive strategies to reduce the likelihood of preterm delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 4 -- Community 2885
retrieved_docs.content: COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY is classified as a subcategory within the broader group of Complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium, indicating that these health issues are primarily associated with the gestational period and its immediate aftermath.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2849
retrieved_docs.content: Poor reproductive history and elective termination of pregnancy are clinically associated with preterm labor (مخاض قبل الأوان). This relationship indicates that women who have experienced a suboptimal reproductive history or who have undergone an elective termination of pregnancy may be at increased risk for developing preterm labor. Recognizing these associations can help clinicians identify patients who require closer monitoring and targeted interventions to prevent or manage preterm labor, thereby improving maternal and neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 109
retrieved_docs.content: disorders, and obstetric interventions together form a comprehensive framework for risk assessment, care planning, and management of maternal‑fetal outcomes during pregnancy, delivery, and the postpartum period.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2071
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy-Related Procedures comprise a range of interventions used to manage complex obstetric conditions and facilitate safe delivery. Among these, the surgical treatment of ectopic pregnancy and abdominal pregnancy represents critical operative approaches for addressing abnormal implantation of the embryo, thereby preventing life‑threatening complications for the mother. In addition, Vaginal Assisted Delivery is included as a procedural option that supports the progression of labor and assists in the birth of the infant when additional support is required. Together, these child procedures under the umbrella of Pregnancy-Related Procedures illustrate the spectrum of surgical and assisted techniques employed in modern obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 209
retrieved_docs.content: constitute major complications of pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium. Recognizing the placement of each specific condition within its broader category supports comprehensive assessment, risk stratification, and coordinated management of pregnant patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2320
retrieved_docs.content: The gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is directly linked to the outcome of a liveborn infant, indicating that reaching this stage of pregnancy can result in a newborn who is alive at birth. In the broader classification of pregnancy timelines, the concept of Duration of gestation encompasses liveborn as a specific category or outcome, reflecting that livebirth is considered one of the possible results within the overall spectrum of gestational durations. This relationship underscores the clinical relevance of gestational age in determining the likelihood of a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: The medical framework links gestational age, birth outcomes, and perinatal disorders through a hierarchical classification system. A gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is identified as a precursor to a liveborn outcome, indicating that infants born after this duration can survive outside the womb. Duration of gestation is positioned as a broader category that inversely encompasses liveborn, underscoring that the length of pregnancy defines the viability of a live birth. Conditions associated with short gestation and low birth weight — collectively described as Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation — serve as a primary classifier for liveborn infants, reflecting the clinical importance of prematurity and insufficient fetal growth in determining neonatal status. This same condition is also classified as ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن} (“birth at the highest degree of prematurity, unspecified weight”), reinforcing its role as a marker of

retrieved_docs.content: essential for clinicians to anticipate potential delivery complications, decide when normal delivery procedures are feasible, and implement appropriate interventions to optimize maternal and neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 1021
retrieved_docs.content: organizes general obstetric procedure categories (إجراء توليدي, Antepartum Procedures, Obstetrics incision) and specific clinical actions—ranging from diagnostic sampling and membrane management to fetal stimulation, reduction of multiple gestations, and surgical treatment of ectopic pregnancies—providing a comprehensive framework for coordinating obstetric care across the ante‑partum, intrapartum, and postpartum periods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1741
retrieved_docs.content: Intrauterine pregnancy is closely linked to a range of obstetric events. It is clinically associated with “other pregnancy and delivery including normal,” indicating that a normally progressing intrauterine gestation often leads to standard delivery outcomes. At the same time, intrauterine pregnancy shows clinical associations with cesarean delivery (الولادة القيصرية), endometritis (الْتِهابُ بِطانَةِ الرَّحِمِ), and the procedure described as ربط البوق, suggesting that these conditions frequently occur together in the context of intrauterine gestation. Cesarean delivery (الولادة القيصرية) itself is clinically associated with preterm birth (ولادة قبل الأوان) and with infant outcomes such as preterm low birth weight (INFANT PRETERM LOW BIRTH WEIGHT), highlighting the potential impact of surgical delivery on neonatal maturity and weight. Additionally, cesarean delivery co‑occurs with ربط البوق, reinforcing the connection between this surgical approach and the aforementioned procedure. “Other pregnancy and delivery including normal” co‑occurs with preterm birth (ولادة قبل الأوان) and also has a relationship with cesarean delivery (الولادة القيصرية), indicating that even pregnancies classified as normal can be linked to early delivery and may sometimes require surgical intervention. The condition described as حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ co‑occurs with ربط البوق, further emphasizing the recurrent association of ربط البوق with various pregnancy‑related states. Overall, the network of relationships demonstrates that intrauterine pregnancy, normal delivery, preterm birth, cesarean delivery, infant preterm low birth weight, endometritis, and the procedure ربط البوق are interwoven in clinical practice, with multiple conditions frequently occurring together or influencing one another, which is essential information for comprehensive obstetric assessment and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 4 -- Community 2769
retrieved_docs.content: Low transverse cesarean section is clinically associated with تمزق الأغشية الباكر (premature rupture of membranes), indicating that this surgical approach to delivery may be linked to an increased occurrence of early membrane rupture, a factor that clinicians must consider when planning obstetric care and managing potential complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 850
retrieved_docs.content: care during pregnancy, labor, and delivery. This integrated framework supports comprehensive obstetric assessment by recognizing how fetal presentation anomalies and maternal surgical history jointly inform the need for specialized care.
